questions.question:
Reference to Context: 'The young seagull was alone on his ledge. His two brothers and his sister had already flown away the day before. He had been afraid to fly with them.'

(i) Where was the young seagull?
(ii) Why was he alone?
(iii) What was the young seagull afraid of?
(iv) What does this opening tell us about the seagull's character?
questions.answer:
(i) The young seagull was alone on his ledge — a narrow surface jutting out from the side of a high cliff by the sea.

(ii) He was alone because his two brothers and his little sister had already flown away from the ledge the day before. They had successfully taken their first flights, but the young seagull had been too afraid to join them.

(iii) The young seagull was afraid of flying. When he had tried to run to the brink of the ledge and flap his wings, the vast expanse of sea far below terrified him. He felt certain that his wings would never support him.

(iv) The opening tells us that the young seagull was timid and fearful despite being physically capable of flight. His wings were actually longer than his siblings', showing that his problem was mental, not physical. He lacked the courage to take a risk.
key_points: Alone on ledge on a cliff
Brothers and sister had already flown
questions.extract: The young seagull was alone on his ledge. His two brothers and his sister had already flown away the day before. He had been afraid to fly with them.

questions.question:
Reference to Context: 'Somehow when he had taken a little run forward to the brink of the ledge and attempted to flap his wings, he became afraid. The great expanse of sea stretched down beneath, and it was such a long way down — miles down.'

(i) What did the young seagull attempt to do?
(ii) Why did he become afraid?
(iii) What does 'expanse' mean?
(iv) Is 'miles down' literally true? What effect does this exaggeration create?
questions.answer:
(i) The young seagull attempted to run forward to the edge of the ledge and flap his wings to take off and fly, just as his brothers and sister had done.

(ii) He became afraid because when he looked down from the brink, he could see the great expanse of sea stretching far below him. The distance from the ledge to the water seemed enormous and terrifying to him. He felt certain that his wings would never support him over such a vast drop.

(iii) 'Expanse' means a large, wide, open area of land, water, or sky. Here it refers to the vast stretch of sea visible below the cliff.

(iv) 'Miles down' is not literally true — it is a hyperbole (exaggeration) used to show how the distance appeared to the frightened young seagull. When we are afraid, things seem much worse than they actually are. This exaggeration effectively conveys the seagull's overwhelming fear and makes the reader understand his terror.
key_points: Attempted to fly from the brink
questions.extract: Somehow when he had taken a little run forward to the brink of the ledge and attempted to flap his wings, he became afraid. The great expanse of sea stretched down beneath, and it was such a long way down — miles down.

questions.question:
Reference to Context: 'Even when each of his brothers and his little sister, whose wings were far shorter than his own, ran to the brink, flapped their wings, and flew away, he failed to muster up courage to take that plunge which appeared to him so desperate.'

(i) What had his brothers and sister managed to do?
(ii) What ironic detail does the author include about the seagull's wings?
(iii) What does 'muster up courage' mean?
(iv) Why does the author call the plunge 'desperate'?
questions.answer:
(i) His brothers and his little sister had successfully run to the brink of the ledge, flapped their wings, and flown away. They had all managed to take their first flight without any difficulty.

(ii) The ironic detail is that the young seagull's wings were actually longer than those of his siblings ('whose wings were far shorter than his own'). This is ironic because physically he was the most capable flyer among them, yet he was the only one who could not fly. His problem was entirely psychological — fear, not physical inadequacy.

(iii) 'Muster up courage' means to make an effort to gather or summon courage from within oneself. It means trying hard to find bravery despite being afraid. The seagull tried but could not find the inner strength to take the leap.

(iv) The author calls the plunge 'desperate' because from the young seagull's perspective, jumping off the ledge seemed like a life-or-death action. The word shows how terrifying and extreme the act of flying appeared to the frightened bird — as if it were a leap into certain danger.
questions.extract: Even when each of his brothers and his little sister, whose wings were far shorter than his own, ran to the brink, flapped their wings, and flew away, he failed to muster up courage to take that plunge which appeared to him so desperate.

questions.question:
Reference to Context: 'His father and mother had come around calling to him shrilly, scolding him, threatening to let him starve on his ledge unless he flew away. But for the life of him he could not move.'

(i) How did the parents try to make the seagull fly?
(ii) What does 'shrilly' mean?
(iii) What does 'for the life of him he could not move' mean?
(iv) What does this tell us about the power of fear?
questions.answer:
(i) The parents tried three approaches to make the seagull fly: first, they called to him shrilly (with loud, high-pitched cries); second, they scolded him for his cowardice; and third, they threatened to let him starve on his ledge unless he flew away. They used encouragement, anger, and threats.

(ii) 'Shrilly' means in a loud, sharp, high-pitched voice. It suggests the parents were crying out urgently and with great intensity, trying to get through to their frightened son.

(iii) 'For the life of him he could not move' is an expression meaning that no matter how hard he tried, he absolutely could not bring himself to move. Even though his life depended on it, the fear was so overpowering that it completely paralysed him.

(iv) This tells us that fear can be so powerful that it overrides even our survival instincts. Despite the threat of starvation and despite knowing that his siblings had flown safely, the seagull's fear was stronger than reason, logic, or even self-preservation. Fear can completely paralyse a person or creature.
key_points: Expression meaning absolutely could not move
questions.extract: His father and mother had come around calling to him shrilly, scolding him, threatening to let him starve on his ledge unless he flew away. But for the life of him he could not move.

questions.question:
Reference to Context: 'He had then trotted back and forth from one end of the ledge to the other, his long grey legs stepping daintily, trying to find some means of reaching his parents without having to fly.'

(i) What was the young seagull trying to do?
(ii) What does 'daintily' mean here?
(iii) Why couldn't he reach his parents without flying?
(iv) What does this behaviour reveal about the seagull?
questions.answer:
(i) The young seagull was trying to find some way to get to his parents without flying. He walked back and forth along the ledge, looking for an alternative path or route that would let him reach the plateau where his family was.

(ii) 'Daintily' means in a delicate and graceful manner. It describes how the seagull was stepping carefully and precisely along the narrow ledge, placing his long grey legs with care.

(iii) He couldn't reach his parents without flying because the ledge ended in a sheer precipice (steep cliff) on each side, with the sea beneath. Between him and his parents there was a deep, wide crack. The cliff face above was sheer and the precipice was farther away than the sea beneath — there was simply no path.

(iv) This behaviour reveals that the seagull was desperately looking for an easy way out — trying to avoid facing his fear rather than confronting it. He wanted the result (reaching his parents) without taking the difficult step (flying). This is a very human behaviour — we often try to find comfortable alternatives rather than face our fears directly.
key_points: Trying to reach parents without flying
Ledge ended in precipices, no path existed
Avoiding fear rather than confronting it
questions.extract: He had then trotted back and forth from one end of the ledge to the other, his long grey legs stepping daintily, trying to find some means of reaching his parents without having to fly.

questions.question:
Reference to Context: ''Ga, ga, ga,' he cried, begging her to bring him over some food. 'Gawl-ool-ah,' she screamed back mockingly. But he kept calling plaintively, and after a minute or so he uttered a joyful scream.'

(i) Who is 'he' and who is 'she'?
(ii) Why was the young seagull crying 'Ga, ga, ga'?
(iii) What does 'mockingly' and 'plaintively' tell us about the exchange?
(iv) Why did the seagull utter a joyful scream?
questions.answer:
(i) 'He' is the young seagull who is stranded on the ledge, and 'she' is his mother who is on the plateau opposite.

(ii) The young seagull was crying 'Ga, ga, ga' because he was desperately hungry. He could see his mother tearing at a piece of fish, and the sight of food maddened him. He was begging her to bring some food over to him.

(iii) 'Mockingly' means in an unkind, mocking manner — the mother screamed back at him as if taunting or teasing him, refusing to show sympathy. 'Plaintively' means in a sad, pleading manner — the young seagull's cries were full of sadness and desperation. The contrast shows the mother was being deliberately tough while the seagull was genuinely suffering.

(iv) The seagull uttered a joyful scream because after his plaintive calling, his mother picked up a piece of fish and started flying across to him with it. He was overjoyed at the prospect of finally getting some food.
questions.extract: 'Ga, ga, ga,' he cried, begging her to bring him over some food. 'Gawl-ool-ah,' she screamed back mockingly. But he kept calling plaintively, and after a minute or so he uttered a joyful scream.

questions.question:
Reference to Context: 'He waited a moment in surprise, wondering why she did not come nearer, and then maddened by hunger, he dived at the fish. With a loud scream he fell outwards and downwards into space.'

(i) Why was the seagull surprised?
(ii) What does 'maddened by hunger' tell us?
(iii) What happened when he dived at the fish?
(iv) Was the mother's trick intentional? How do you know?
questions.answer:
(i) The seagull was surprised because his mother had stopped just opposite him with the fish almost within reach of his beak but did not come any closer. She halted in mid-air with her wings motionless, dangling the food tantalisingly close but not close enough for him to grab.

(ii) 'Maddened by hunger' tells us that the seagull was driven to the point of desperation by his extreme hunger. He had not eaten for over twenty-four hours, and the sight of food so close was unbearable. His hunger overpowered his fear — the same fear that had kept him frozen for a full day.

(iii) When he dived at the fish, he fell outwards and downwards into the space below the ledge. This was the crucial moment — his lunge for food sent him off the ledge and into the air for the first time.

(iv) Yes, the mother's trick was entirely intentional. She deliberately stopped just out of reach, knowing that the hungry seagull would lean forward or dive for the food and end up in the air. This was her clever strategy to force him to fly — she used his hunger to overcome his fear.
key_points: Mother stopped just out of reach
Diving for fish pushed him off the ledge
questions.extract: He waited a moment in surprise, wondering why she did not come nearer, and then maddened by hunger, he dived at the fish. With a loud scream he fell outwards and downwards into space.

questions.question:
Reference to Context: 'Then a monstrous terror seized him and his heart stood still. He could hear nothing. But it only lasted a moment. The next moment he felt his wings spread outwards.'

(i) What 'monstrous terror' seized the seagull?
(ii) What does 'his heart stood still' mean?
(iii) How long did the terror last?
(iv) What happened next and what does it signify?
questions.answer:
(i) The 'monstrous terror' was the overwhelming fear that seized the young seagull the moment he fell off the ledge into the air. He was plunging downwards and the full horror of what was happening — that he was falling through space — gripped him.

(ii) 'His heart stood still' is an expression meaning he was paralysed with extreme fear. It felt as though his heart stopped beating for a moment because the terror was so intense. It conveys a moment of complete shock and dread.

(iii) The terror lasted only a moment. Despite being so intense, it was very brief. This shows how quickly things changed from fear to flight.

(iv) The next moment, he felt his wings spread outwards. The wind rushed against his breast feathers, under his stomach, and against his wings. He was not falling — he was soaring. This signifies the turning point of the story: the moment the seagull discovered that his wings could indeed support him. His fear had been baseless all along, and the instant he was forced to use his wings, they worked perfectly.
key_points: Heart stood still = paralysed with fear
questions.extract: Then a monstrous terror seized him and his heart stood still. He could hear nothing. But it only lasted a moment. The next moment he felt his wings spread outwards.

questions.question:
Reference to Context: 'Then he completely forgot that he had not always been able to fly, and commenced himself to dive and soar, shrieking shrilly.'

(i) What did the young seagull forget?
(ii) What does 'commenced' mean?
(iii) What was he now able to do?
(iv) What is the significance of this line in the story?
questions.answer:
(i) The young seagull forgot that he had not always been able to fly. He forgot his long hours of fear, his inability to take the plunge, and his time stranded on the ledge. The joy of flying completely erased the memory of his terror.

(ii) 'Commenced' means began or started. He started to dive and soar on his own, actively enjoying the act of flying rather than just trying to survive.

(iii) He was now able to dive and soar freely, shrieking shrilly with delight. He was flying confidently, performing the same moves he had watched his family do — the very things he had been too afraid to attempt just moments earlier.

(iv) This line is highly significant because it shows how completely fear can disappear once we face it. The seagull's fear had been all-consuming for over twenty-four hours, yet within moments of actually flying, it vanished entirely. The message is powerful — our fears often seem enormous before we face them, but once we take the first step, we may discover that what we feared was never real at all.
key_points: Forgot his earlier inability to fly
questions.extract: Then he completely forgot that he had not always been able to fly, and commenced himself to dive and soar, shrieking shrilly.

questions.question:
Reference to Context: 'He was floating on it. And around him his family was screaming, praising him, and their beaks were offering him scraps of dog-fish. He had made his first flight.'

(i) What was the seagull floating on?
(ii) How did his family react?
(iii) What is the significance of offering him food?
(iv) What does the last sentence mean in a deeper sense?
questions.answer:
(i) The seagull was floating on the sea. After flying over the ocean, he had landed on the water. His feet and then his belly sank into the green sea, but he did not sink further — he floated.

(ii) His family reacted with joy and pride. They were screaming and praising him, celebrating his achievement. They offered him scraps of dog-fish, finally feeding the bird who had been starving for over twenty-four hours.

(iii) The offering of food is significant because throughout the story, the family had deliberately withheld food to force the seagull to fly. Now that he had overcome his fear and made his first flight, they rewarded him with food. It symbolises acceptance, celebration, and the end of his ordeal. The food represents their approval and love.

(iv) 'He had made his first flight' is more than just a literal statement about a bird flying. On a deeper level, it represents overcoming one's fears and achieving something that seemed impossible. The 'first flight' symbolises any first step we take despite being afraid — whether it's speaking in public, trying something new, or standing up for ourselves. The story tells us that courage is not the absence of fear but the will to act despite it.
questions.extract: He was floating on it. And around him his family was screaming, praising him, and their beaks were offering him scraps of dog-fish. He had made his first flight.
